subject: ap-comparative-government
theme: Electoral rules, party systems, and citizen organizations are never neutral — every rule governing who can run, how votes are counted, and which groups can organize reflects and reinforces the underlying power structure of the regime.

core_concepts.title: Electoral Systems: SMD/FPTP vs. Proportional Representation
core_concepts.detail: Two major electoral systems produce different political outcomes. Single-member district, first-past-the-post (FPTP): one representative per district, whoever gets the most votes wins — no majority required. The UK uses FPTP exclusively for the House of Commons. Effects: tends to produce two-party dominant systems (major parties are over-rewarded, minor parties under-rewarded), strong constituency accountability (one representative answers to each district), and strategic/tactical voting (voters may vote for a less-preferred candidate to block their least-preferred). Proportional representation (PR): parties win seats proportional to their vote share, using multimember districts. Effects: promotes multiparty systems, increases minority and women's representation, reduces wasted votes. Russia uses a mixed system (half SMD, half PR with a threshold). Mexico uses a mixed system (300 SMD + 200 PR seats in the Chamber of Deputies). Nigeria uses SMD for its House of Representatives. China uses indirect elections for the NPC — citizens vote for local delegates, who vote for higher-level delegates, ultimately selecting NPC members.

core_concepts.title: Presidential Election Rules by Country
core_concepts.detail: Presidential election rules differ significantly across the course countries. Mexico: plurality rule — the candidate with the most votes wins, even without a majority; no runoff. Nigeria: unique geographic threshold — the winner must receive the most votes AND secure at least 25% of the vote in two-thirds (24 of 36) of Nigeria's states, reflecting the federal structure and requiring national breadth of support. Iran and Russia: absolute majority rule — a candidate must win more than 50% of the vote; if no candidate achieves this in the first round, a runoff is held between the top two vote earners. The geographic threshold is Nigeria's most distinctive electoral rule and one of the most heavily tested in Unit 4. The INE (National Electoral Institute) in Mexico and the INEC in Nigeria are independent election management bodies created during democratic transitions to reduce fraud and enhance electoral competition.

core_concepts.title: Party Systems by Country
core_concepts.detail: Party systems range from one-party monopoly to genuine multiparty competition. China: formally a one-party state; the Communist Party of China (CPC/CCP) has ruled since 1949; eight minor parties exist legally but operate under CPC supervision in consultative roles only, holding no independent power. Russia: dominant party system engineered by the state — United Russia dominates through six mechanisms: increasing party registration requirements, using courts to disqualify candidates, limiting opposition media access, raising threshold rules, eliminating then reinstating gubernatorial elections, and creating federal district presidential envoys. Iran: no formal party structures — parties operate as loosely formed alliances or factions; the Guardian Council vets all candidates for the Majles and presidency, excluding reformists and those who do not support Islamic values. Mexico: multiparty competition with three historically CED-named major parties — PAN (National Action Party), PRD (Party of the Democratic Revolution), and PRI (Institutional Revolutionary Party). Contemporary note: Morena emerged as a dominant force by 2018–2024. Nigeria: multiparty system with two dominant parties — the People's Democratic Party (PDP, held presidency 1999–2015) and the All Progressives Congress (APC, won landmark 2015 election); ethnic quotas affect representation. UK: two-party tendency (Conservative and Labour dominate) driven by FPTP rules; regional parties (SNP in Scotland, Plaid Cymru in Wales) win seats through geographic vote concentration.

core_concepts.title: Mexico's Democratic Transition and El Dedazo
core_concepts.detail: Mexico's shift from PRI one-party dominance to multiparty competition is one of the most important democratization case studies in the course. The PRI ruled Mexico from 1929 to 2000 under a corporatist structure — the state licensed and controlled organized interests (labor, business, peasants) through single peak associations, channeling them into the party. El dedazo was the informal practice by which the sitting PRI president personally hand-picked (pointed his finger at) the next presidential candidate — effectively making the selection before any election occurred. Mexico's democratization involved eliminating el dedazo, privatizing state-owned corporations (weakening the PRI's patronage base), decentralizing power to subnational governments, and creating the INE/IFE as an independent electoral authority. The PAN victory in 2000 under Vicente Fox ended 71 years of unbroken PRI rule — the CED's landmark transfer of power example. Mexico then transitioned from a corporatist interest group system (state-controlled) toward a pluralist system (autonomous groups competing freely).

core_concepts.title: Social Movements and Interest Groups
core_concepts.detail: Social movements involve large, often decentralized groups pushing collectively for significant political or social change. Interest groups are formally organized to advocate for a specific policy or constituency. The CED names four specific social movement cases. Iran's Green Movement (2009): mass protests following the disputed re-election of Mahmoud Ahmadinejad, alleging widespread fraud; the movement was suppressed by state security forces. Mexico's Zapatista (EZLN) uprising (1994): armed indigenous rebellion in Chiapas protesting socioeconomic inequality and the effects of NAFTA; became a broader movement demanding indigenous land rights and autonomy. Nigeria's MEND (Movement for the Emancipation of the Niger Delta): militant group that attacked oil infrastructure and demanded revenue redistribution for Niger Delta communities. Nigeria's MOSOP (Movement for the Survival of the Ogoni People): non-violent advocacy group led by Ken Saro-Wiwa, who was executed by the Sani Abacha military government in 1995 after leading campaigns against Shell Oil's extraction practices — the highest-yield Nigeria person card in the course. Social movements are difficult for governments to suppress fully because of their decentralized structure, but they are also hard to sustain and negotiate with for the same reason.

core_concepts.title: Pluralism vs. Corporatism
core_concepts.detail: Two models describe how governments relate to organized interest groups. Pluralism: multiple autonomous interest groups compete freely for policy influence without state direction or licensing — the state does not designate which groups may participate. The UK is the primary pluralist example. Pluralist systems are associated with democratic regimes. Corporatism: the government controls access to policy making by officially recognizing specific state-sanctioned groups — typically one per sector (labor, business, agriculture) called single peak associations (SPAs). Only these licensed groups may represent their sectors in policy making. The state retains more control over citizen input. Mexico under the PRI is the CED's named example of a corporatist system. Mexico's transition away from corporatism toward pluralism is directly cited in the CED as a case study in how interest group systems change over time. The key exam distinction: in pluralism, groups form independently and compete; in corporatism, the state structures which groups exist and who they represent.

core_concepts.title: Iran's Guardian Council as Electoral Gatekeeper
core_concepts.detail: Iran's Guardian Council is a 12-member body (6 Islamic jurists appointed by the Supreme Leader + 6 civil lawyers nominated by the head of judiciary with Majles approval) that performs two key electoral functions in Unit 4. First, it vets all candidates for the Majles and the presidency, excluding reform-minded candidates and those who do not demonstrate sufficient support for Islamic values — this fundamentally restricts electoral competition and makes Iranian elections non-competitive in a democratic sense. Second, it reviews all legislation passed by the Majles to ensure compliance with Islamic law, and can block legislation. When disputes arise between the Majles and the Guardian Council, the Expediency Council (appointed by the Supreme Leader) resolves them. Iran thus lacks formal political party structures — political groups operate as loose factions — and elections are managed by an unelected clerical body rather than an independent electoral commission.

exam_tip: Unit 4 is heavily comparative: the exam will ask you to explain why specific electoral rules produce specific outcomes (FPTP in the UK tends toward a two-party system; Nigeria's geographic threshold reflects federalism), to distinguish pluralism from corporatism and identify which country exemplifies each, and to explain the six mechanisms Russia uses to maintain dominant-party control — candidate disqualification, registration barriers, media limits, threshold rules, selective court decisions, and regional envoy appointments.
